title: pip & Real Libraries (Mini-Project)
subtitle: Meet the giant toolbox of Python — then build a real game with what you already know.
goal: Understand pip, PyPI, and famous libraries, then build a complete Number Guessing Game using only the standard library.

blocks.heading: The whole Python world 🌍
blocks.html:
So far you've used Python's built-in tools and the standard library (handy modules like random and math that come free with Python). But there's a HUGE world beyond that.

All over the planet, people write Python code and share it for free. You can grab their code and use it in your own programs — no need to build everything from scratch. Today you learn how that works, then you build a real game! 🎉

blocks.heading: What is pip? 📦
blocks.html:
pip is Python's package installer. A package (also called a library) is a bundle of code someone else wrote so you don't have to. With one short command you download it and start using it.

On a real computer (not this browser) you'd open a terminal and type something like pip install requests. That fetches the package and gets it ready to import.

Heads up: pip install only works in a real terminal on your computer — it can't run inside this browser. So in this lesson, pip commands are shown as text to read, and every box you can press Run on uses only the standard library. 🙂

blocks.heading: What is PyPI? 🏬
blocks.html: PyPI (say it "pie-pee-eye") stands for the Python Package Index. Think of it as a giant free app store for Python code, living at pypi.org. When you run pip install something, pip goes to PyPI, finds that package, and downloads it for you. There are hundreds of thousands of packages waiting there!

blocks.heading: Famous libraries (and what they do) ⭐
blocks.html:
Here are some superstar libraries people install with pip:

  • requests — grabs pages and data from the internet, like a friendly web fetcher.
  • pandas — works with tables of data, like a super-powered spreadsheet.
  • numpy — does fast math on big lists of numbers.
  • flask and django — build websites and web apps.
  • pygame — make games with pictures, sound, and movement.
  • matplotlib — draw charts and graphs from your data.

You'd install any of them like this:

pip install pandas
You don't have to memorize these! Just know the kind of thing each does, so when you need it later you'll remember "oh, there's a library for that." 🔎

blocks.heading: Virtual environments (keeping projects tidy) 🧹
blocks.html:
Imagine each project living in its own clean box, with its very own set of libraries. That box is called a virtual environment. It stops one project's libraries from clashing with another's — like keeping your LEGO sets in separate bins so the pieces don't get mixed up.

On a real computer you'd make one with python -m venv myenv and turn it on before installing packages. You don't need it today, but now you know the word when you see it later. 👍

blocks.heading: Mini-Project time: a Number Guessing Game! 🎯
blocks.html:
Now the fun part. We'll build a real Number Guessing Game using only the standard library — so it runs right here in your browser. The computer picks a secret number, and we guess until we get it.

We'll use everything you've learned: the random module, variables, if/elif/else, loops, and functions. Let's build it piece by piece. 🛠️

blocks.caption: Piece 1: pick a secret number with random
blocks.code:
import random

secret = random.randint(1, 10)
print("I picked a secret number between 1 and 10.")
print("(Shhh, it is:", secret, ")")
blocks.explain: import random gives us the random module from the standard library. random.randint(1, 10) picks a whole number from 1 to 10 (both ends included). Right now we print the secret just so we can see it works — in the real game we'll keep it hidden!

blocks.caption: Piece 2: compare a guess to the secret
blocks.explain: This is the brain of the game. We compare the guess to the secret with ==, < and >. Change the value of guess to 7 and press Run again — you'll get the winning message!

blocks.caption: Piece 3: loop through several guesses
blocks.explain: A for loop checks each guess in our list one by one. We put secret as the last item so the game always ends with a win. In a real game the guesses would come from a player typing them in!

blocks.html: In a real terminal you'd use input() to ask the player for a guess. The browser runner can't pause for typing, so here we use lists of guesses to test our logic. The thinking is exactly the same! 🧠

blocks.question: What does pip do?
options: Makes your code run faster automatically
blocks.explain: pip is Python's package installer. pip install <name> downloads a library from PyPI so you can use it.

blocks.question: Which library would you reach for to build a game with pictures and sound?
blocks.explain: pygame is made for games with graphics, sound, and movement. pandas is for data tables, requests is for the web, and matplotlib draws charts.

blocks.question: Why do people use a virtual environment?
options: To keep each project's libraries in their own tidy box so they don't clash
To make the computer turn off faster
To delete old packages from PyPI
To turn Python code into a website
blocks.explain: A virtual environment keeps one project's libraries separate from another's — like separate bins for separate LEGO sets.

blocks.title: Pick your own secret number
blocks.instructions: Use the random module to pick a secret number between 1 and 100, then print it. Make sure you import random and use random.randint.
blocks.starter:
import random

# TODO: use random.randint(1, 100) to pick the secret
secret = 0
print("My secret number is", secret)
blocks.solution:
import random

secret = random.randint(1, 100)
print("My secret number is", secret)
blocks.passMsg: Nice — random.randint is now in your toolbox! 🎲
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you have 'import random' and use random.randint(1, 100).

blocks.title: Write the guess-checker
blocks.instructions: Finish the if/elif/else so it prints 'Too low!' when the guess is smaller than secret, 'Too high!' when it's bigger, and 'Correct!' when they match.
blocks.starter:
secret = 42
guess = 30

if guess == secret:
    print("Correct!")
# add elif and else below
blocks.passMsg: That's the heart of the game beating! ❤️
blocks.failMsg: You need all three: if, elif, and else, to cover correct / too low / too high.

challenge.title: Build the full Number Guessing Game 🏆
challenge.instructions: Put the pieces together into one program. Write a function play_game() that: picks a secret number with random.randint, loops through a list of guesses, and for each guess prints 'too low', 'too high', or 'you win'. Then call play_game(). Use import random, a function (def), a loop (for), and if/elif/else.
challenge.starter:
import random

def play_game():
    secret = random.randint(1, 10)
    guesses = [3, 7, 5, secret]
    # loop through guesses and check each one

play_game()
challenge.hint: Inside the loop, use 'if guess == secret' for the win, 'elif guess < secret' for too low, and 'else' for too high.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built a real game from scratch! You now know pip, PyPI, and the giant world of Python libraries — you can build real things now. Incredible work finishing Day 21!
